models.name: CAP Theorem
models.first_principle: In a distributed system, you can only guarantee two of three properties simultaneously: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
models.derived_from: Fundamental limits of network reliability and synchronization
models.when_applies: Designing distributed databases, microservices data stores, or any system where multiple nodes must agree on state
models.when_breaks_down: Single-node systems; when partition tolerance isn't required (e.g., systems on a single reliable LAN)
models.ai_application: Vector databases and embedding stores face CAP tradeoffs — eventual consistency in a multi-region Bedrock Knowledge Base can return stale embeddings during partition events

models.name: Conway's Law
models.first_principle: Systems mirror the communication structures of the organizations that build them.
models.derived_from: Organizations optimize for internal communication paths; software boundaries emerge along team boundaries
models.when_applies: Designing microservice boundaries, API contracts, team topologies, or any large-scale system with multiple teams
models.when_breaks_down: Small teams building small systems; when strong technical leadership overrides org structure intentionally
models.ai_application: AI agent boundaries often reflect team ownership — an 'asset agent' owned by one team won't naturally share context with a 'workflow agent' owned by another without explicit integration design

models.name: Chesterton's Fence
models.first_principle: Don't remove something until you understand why it was put there.
models.derived_from: Survivorship bias + accumulated context; the absence of visible failure doesn't mean no protection exists
models.when_applies: Refactoring legacy systems, deprecating APIs, removing 'dead' code, simplifying prompt chains
models.when_breaks_down: When original builders were provably wrong; when context has fundamentally changed and the fence is now actively harmful
models.ai_application: Don't strip 'redundant' guardrails in a system prompt without knowing what failure mode they were preventing. That extra instruction probably exists because Claude did something unexpected once.

models.name: Leaky Abstractions
models.first_principle: All non-trivial abstractions leak — the details they hide will surface at the worst possible moment.
models.derived_from: Joel Spolsky; abstraction layers cannot eliminate underlying complexity, only defer it
models.when_applies: Choosing frameworks, ORMs, managed services, or any layer that hides implementation details
models.when_breaks_down: Truly thin wrappers with no hidden state; simple pass-through adapters
models.ai_application: Bedrock Knowledge Bases abstract chunking, embedding, and retrieval — but when retrieval quality degrades, you must understand the underlying mechanics. The abstraction leaks at the seams.

models.name: Fallacies of Dist. Computing
models.first_principle: Eight assumptions developers wrongly make: network is reliable, latency is zero, bandwidth is infinite, network is secure, topology doesn't change, there is one admin, transport cost is zero, network is homogeneous.
models.derived_from: Peter Deutsch; gaps between mental models and physical reality in networked systems
models.when_applies: Any system that makes network calls — microservices, agents calling tools, RAG pipelines, multi-region deployments
models.when_breaks_down: Single-process systems; embedded systems with no network dependency
models.ai_application: AI agents that call external tools assume network reliability. An agentic loop that retries on failure without idempotency guards will corrupt state when the network is merely slow, not down.

models.name: Two-Pizza Rule
models.first_principle: If a team can't be fed by two pizzas, it's too big. Communication overhead scales as O(n²) with team size.
models.derived_from: Jeff Bezos; combinatorial explosion of communication paths degrades coordination efficiency
models.when_applies: Designing team structures, microservice ownership, agent crew sizes, context window allocation
models.when_breaks_down: Tasks requiring rare expertise that can't be distributed; projects requiring tight coupling between many specialists
models.ai_application: Multi-agent systems face the same n² communication cost — a crew of 10 agents with shared state has 45 interaction paths. Design agent graphs with small, focused crews and explicit handoff contracts.

models.name: Amdahl's Law
models.first_principle: The speedup from parallelizing a task is limited by its sequential fraction. If 20% must run serially, max speedup is 5x regardless of cores.
models.derived_from: Gene Amdahl; fundamental limit on parallelism imposed by dependencies
models.when_applies: Evaluating parallelism strategies, multi-agent fan-out, horizontal scaling, batch processing pipelines
models.when_breaks_down: Embarrassingly parallel problems with no sequential dependencies (e.g., batch embedding generation)
models.ai_application: A RAG pipeline with sequential steps (chunk → embed → retrieve → rerank → generate) has a hard latency floor even with infinite parallelism on individual steps. Identify and attack the sequential bottleneck first.

models.name: Little's Law
models.first_principle: L = λW. The average number of items in a system equals the arrival rate multiplied by the average time each item spends in the system.
models.derived_from: John Little; a queuing theory result that holds for any stable system regardless of arrival/service distribution
models.when_applies: Capacity planning, SLA design, sizing thread pools and connection pools, estimating agent concurrency needs
models.when_breaks_down: Unstable systems where arrival rate exceeds service rate (the queue grows without bound)
models.ai_application: If your Bedrock inference endpoint handles 10 req/s and average latency is 3s, you have 30 in-flight requests at steady state. Size your concurrency limits and token budgets accordingly.

models.name: Second System Effect
models.first_principle: Engineers who succeed with a small first system tend to over-engineer the second, loading it with features deferred from the first.
models.derived_from: Fred Brooks, The Mythical Man-Month; overconfidence after initial success breeds scope creep and complexity
models.when_applies: Major rewrites, v2 architecture design, migrating from a POC to a production system
models.when_breaks_down: Teams with strong discipline and explicit anti-scope-creep constraints; when the first system was genuinely too limited
models.ai_application: POC AI architectures that work well tempt architects to add every deferred feature (memory, multi-tenancy, streaming, RLHF) in a single v2 sprint. Ship a walking skeleton first.

models.name: Circuit Breaker
models.first_principle: When a dependency fails repeatedly, stop calling it temporarily. Give it time to recover rather than amplifying failures with retries.
models.derived_from: Michael Nygard, Release It!; electrical circuit breakers that prevent wiring fires by interrupting overload
models.when_applies: Any system calling external dependencies — APIs, databases, AI inference endpoints, downstream services
models.when_breaks_down: Idempotent read-only operations where retrying is harmless; internal function calls
models.ai_application: Agentic tool-use loops that retry on failure can overwhelm a rate-limited Bedrock endpoint. A circuit breaker prevents cascading failure when the model is throttled.

models.name: Eventual Consistency
models.first_principle: Given no new updates, all replicas will converge to the same value — eventually. The system trades strong consistency for availability and partition tolerance.
models.derived_from: Werner Vogels / Dynamo paper; a deliberate design choice accepting temporary divergence for system-wide availability
models.when_applies: Distributed caches, multi-region deployments, event-driven systems, AI memory stores updated asynchronously
models.when_breaks_down: Financial transactions, inventory systems, or any domain where stale reads cause irreversible harm
models.ai_application: Agent memory systems that write asynchronously are eventually consistent. An agent reading its own recent writes may get stale results — design read-after-write flows carefully.

models.name: Goodhart's Law
models.first_principle: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
models.derived_from: Charles Goodhart; optimizing for a proxy metric causes the proxy to decouple from the underlying goal
models.when_applies: Designing evaluation frameworks, reward functions, KPIs, SLAs, or any system that optimizes toward a measurable proxy
models.when_breaks_down: When the measure IS the goal (e.g., counting exact transactions processed)
models.ai_application: Optimizing an AI agent for 'task completion rate' causes it to game easy tasks. RLHF models optimized on human preference ratings learn to produce confident-sounding wrong answers. Measure what you care about, not what's easy to measure.

models.name: Distribution Shift
models.first_principle: A model trained on distribution P will degrade when deployed on distribution Q. The gap between training and production data is the primary source of model failure in production.
models.derived_from: Statistical learning theory; generalization requires i.i.d. assumption that production routinely violates
models.when_applies: Any ML/AI system in production; RAG systems as the knowledge base grows; embedding models as domain terminology evolves
models.when_breaks_down: Truly static, closed-world problems where training and inference data are drawn from the same fixed distribution
models.ai_application: A Bedrock Knowledge Base embedding model trained on general text degrades as your corpus adds specialized domain terminology. Monitor embedding quality over time, not just at launch.

models.name: Exploration-Exploitation
models.first_principle: Any agent with limited time must balance exploiting known good actions against exploring potentially better unknown ones. You cannot fully do both.
models.derived_from: Multi-armed bandit problem in reinforcement learning; generalizes to any resource-constrained search
models.when_applies: AI agent action selection, retrieval strategy design (broad vs. precise search), hyperparameter tuning, feature development prioritization
models.when_breaks_down: Single-shot decisions with no learning opportunity; fully enumerable action spaces
models.ai_application: A RAG retrieval strategy that only exploits high-similarity chunks misses relevant context in lower-ranked results. Hybrid search (dense + sparse) is an exploration mechanism.

models.name: Local vs. Global Optima
models.first_principle: Local optimization can prevent reaching a global optimum. The best solution from where you stand is often not the best solution overall.
models.derived_from: Optimization theory / gradient descent; systems that improve locally can become trapped in suboptimal equilibria
models.when_applies: Architecture decisions under constraints, incremental refactoring, prompt engineering, hyperparameter search, organizational change
models.when_breaks_down: Convex optimization problems where any local optimum is global; when the search space is small enough to enumerate
models.ai_application: Incrementally tuning a RAG pipeline (chunk size, k, reranker threshold) can trap you in a local optimum. Sometimes you need to change the retrieval strategy wholesale — e.g., switching from semantic to hybrid search.

models.name: Survivorship Bias
models.first_principle: We observe survivors, not failures. Conclusions drawn from visible successes systematically ignore the failed cases that would change the inference.
models.derived_from: Abraham Wald / WWII aircraft armor study; selection effects distort the data we reason from
models.when_applies: Post-mortems, benchmark comparisons, architecture pattern selection, evaluating AI outputs, model selection
models.when_breaks_down: Controlled experiments with random assignment that explicitly sample from both success and failure
models.ai_application: Benchmarks for LLM performance measure on curated test sets — the 'survivors' of data curation. Production failure modes are systematically absent. Always complement benchmarks with adversarial red-teaming.

models.name: Two Generals Problem
models.first_principle: It is impossible to achieve guaranteed consensus between two parties over an unreliable channel. Any acknowledgment can itself be lost.
models.derived_from: Formal computer science result; proves that perfect coordination over unreliable networks is mathematically impossible
models.when_applies: Distributed transactions, exactly-once delivery guarantees, agent handoff protocols, multi-step saga patterns
models.when_breaks_down: Reliable synchronous channels (in-process calls); when at-least-once or at-most-once semantics are acceptable
models.ai_application: When an AI agent hands off a task to a sub-agent over an async channel, you cannot guarantee exactly-once execution. Design for idempotency — safe to retry — rather than exactly-once.

models.name: The Alignment Tax
models.first_principle: Safety and alignment constraints reduce raw capability. Every guardrail, refusal, or behavioral constraint is a deliberate capability tradeoff.
models.derived_from: AI safety research; RLHF and constitutional AI introduce constraints that reduce performance on unconstrained benchmarks
models.when_applies: Choosing models, designing system prompts, evaluating tradeoffs between helpfulness and safety, setting guardrails
models.when_breaks_down: When alignment methods improve capability (e.g., chain-of-thought reasoning improves both safety and accuracy)
models.ai_application: A Claude model with a restrictive system prompt will refuse some valid enterprise requests. Design your guardrails to be domain-specific, not maximally restrictive — over-constraining is a reliability failure.

models.name: Feedback Loops
models.first_principle: A system whose output feeds back as input creates amplifying (positive) or stabilizing (negative) loops. Positive loops accelerate; negative loops regulate.
models.derived_from: Control theory / cybernetics; applies universally to any system with output-to-input coupling
models.when_applies: Recommender systems, AI training pipelines, auto-scaling systems, event-driven architectures, model fine-tuning on production data
models.when_breaks_down: Open-loop systems with no feedback path; when loop latency is so long the system is effectively open-loop
models.ai_application: Fine-tuning a model on its own production outputs creates a positive feedback loop — errors compound. RAG systems that update their knowledge base from model-generated summaries face the same risk.

models.name: Zero-One-Infinity
models.first_principle: Allow zero, one, or unlimited instances of something. Any other specific limit is arbitrary and will become a constraint you'll regret.
models.derived_from: Willem van der Poel; arbitrary limits encode hidden assumptions that leak into future constraints
models.when_applies: API design, multi-tenancy limits, prompt template slots, agent tool counts, context window management
models.when_breaks_down: Hard physical limits (e.g., context windows); regulatory requirements imposing specific counts
models.ai_application: If your RAG system supports 'up to 5 document types,' you will add a 6th. Design the pipeline for N document types from the start, with a registry pattern rather than hardcoded branches.

models.name: Pareto Principle
models.first_principle: Roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes. Input and output are not uniformly distributed — a small subset of inputs dominates outcomes.
models.derived_from: Vilfredo Pareto's observation that 80% of Italian land was owned by 20% of people; generalized across economics, engineering, and natural systems
models.when_applies: Prioritizing engineering effort, diagnosing performance bottlenecks, deciding which features to build, allocating testing coverage, triaging incidents
models.when_breaks_down: Safety-critical systems where tail-case failures are catastrophic; regulated domains where all cases must be handled equally; adversarial environments where attackers target the neglected 80%
models.ai_application: 20% of query types account for 80% of your RAG pipeline's failures. Profile before optimizing — fixing the top failure bucket often collapses the error rate more than rewriting the whole pipeline.

models.name: Theory of Constraints
models.first_principle: Every system has exactly one constraint that limits its throughput. Improving anything that is not the constraint improves nothing.
models.derived_from: Eliyahu Goldratt, The Goal; the weakest link in a chain determines its total strength regardless of how strong the others are
models.when_applies: Pipeline optimization, incident response, capacity planning, prioritizing engineering work, debugging latency in multi-step systems
models.when_breaks_down: When the constraint shifts faster than you can address it; highly parallel systems with no shared bottleneck
models.ai_application: In a RAG pipeline, if retrieval takes 800ms and generation takes 200ms, optimizing the LLM call gives you nothing. Identify the constraint first — it's almost always retrieval or chunking quality, not generation speed.

models.name: Separation of Concerns
models.first_principle: A system is easier to reason about and change when each component has one clearly bounded responsibility, with minimal knowledge of other components.
models.derived_from: Edsger Dijkstra; complexity grows when a single module must reason about multiple domains simultaneously
models.when_applies: Service boundary design, prompt architecture, agent tool design, data pipeline structure, API contract definition
models.when_breaks_down: Performance-critical paths where crossing boundaries is too expensive; very small systems where the overhead of separation exceeds the benefit
models.ai_application: A system prompt that handles persona, output format, safety rules, domain knowledge, and tool instructions in one block is hard to debug and update. Separate concerns: use structured sections or layered prompts. Each agent in a crew should own exactly one concern.

models.name: Single Responsibility
models.first_principle: A module should have one and only one reason to change. Coupling multiple responsibilities means a change to either forces changes to both.
models.derived_from: Robert C. Martin; a special case of separation of concerns focused on the axis of change rather than cognitive load
models.when_applies: Designing functions, microservices, Lambda handlers, agents, tool definitions, data models
models.when_breaks_down: Extremely simple scripts where splitting creates more overhead than it saves; tightly coupled domain operations that genuinely change together
models.ai_application: An agent that both decides what to do AND executes it AND logs it AND validates results is hard to test, debug, or swap out. Design narrow agents: one decides, another executes, another validates.

models.name: Postel's Law
models.first_principle: Be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept. Robust systems tolerate imperfect inputs without breaking, but produce well-formed outputs.
models.derived_from: Jon Postel, RFC 793 (TCP); designed to keep the internet working despite heterogeneous, imperfect implementations
models.when_applies: API design, message schema evolution, inter-service contracts, parsing user input, designing agent tool interfaces
models.when_breaks_down: Security-sensitive inputs where accepting anything enables injection attacks; when liberal acceptance masks bugs in callers and breeds long-term protocol entropy
models.ai_application: LLM outputs are liberal producers — they rarely produce perfectly structured JSON. Your parsing layer must be robust to minor format deviations. But your agent's tool outputs should be strictly typed so downstream agents can rely on them.

models.name: Idempotency
models.first_principle: An operation that produces the same result whether applied once or many times is safe to retry. Without idempotency, retries cause duplication or corruption.
models.derived_from: Mathematics (idempotent functions); foundational to reliable distributed systems — retries are inevitable, so operations must be safe to repeat
models.when_applies: Any operation that can be retried — API calls, event handlers, database writes, agent tool calls, payment processing, message consumers
models.when_breaks_down: Pure reads (already safe); true one-time events where idempotency is impossible and exactly-once delivery must be guaranteed at the infrastructure level
models.ai_application: Agentic tool calls will be retried — by the agent, by the orchestrator, or by infrastructure. A non-idempotent tool call (e.g., 'send email') will send duplicate emails. Every tool an agent can call should either be idempotent or carry an explicit deduplication key.

models.name: Event-Driven Architecture
models.first_principle: Components communicate by emitting and reacting to events rather than calling each other directly. The producer doesn't know who consumes; the consumer doesn't know who produced.
models.derived_from: Observer pattern / reactive systems manifesto; decoupling via an event bus reduces direct dependencies and enables async processing
models.when_applies: Systems requiring loose coupling between services, async processing, fan-out to multiple consumers, audit trails, temporal decoupling
models.when_breaks_down: When you need synchronous request-response semantics; when event ordering guarantees are required but hard to enforce; when debugging event chains becomes opaque
models.ai_application: Agentic pipelines map naturally to event-driven patterns — an agent emitting a 'document-processed' event decouples ingestion from indexing from notification. But debugging a hallucination in a 6-event chain is significantly harder than a synchronous call.

models.name: Observability Triangle
models.first_principle: You cannot debug what you cannot observe. Logs tell you what happened. Metrics tell you how much. Traces tell you where. All three are required; any one alone is insufficient.
models.derived_from: Distributed systems practice (Charity Majors et al.); the 3 pillars of observability — logs, metrics, traces — each answer different questions about system behavior
models.when_applies: Any production system; especially multi-step pipelines, microservices, agentic workflows, and AI inference chains where failure modes are emergent
models.when_breaks_down: Simple scripts or single-process systems where stdout is sufficient; extremely cost-sensitive deployments where full observability is prohibitive
models.ai_application: An agentic pipeline without traces is a black box. When Claude returns a wrong answer, you need to know which tool call failed, which retrieved chunk was misleading, and which prompt template was active. Instrument every hop, not just the final output.

models.name: Defence in Depth
models.first_principle: No single security control is sufficient. Layer multiple independent controls so that the failure of any one does not compromise the system.
models.derived_from: Military strategy (castle walls + moat + keep); applied to information security — each layer catches what the previous missed
models.when_applies: Security architecture, prompt injection defence, AI guardrail design, IAM policy design, multi-tenant data isolation
models.when_breaks_down: When layers are not truly independent (a single exploit breaks all layers); when the cost of layering exceeds the risk it mitigates
models.ai_application: A single system prompt guardrail is insufficient defence against prompt injection. Layer: input validation → system prompt → output filtering → downstream action validation. Each layer catches a different attack vector.

models.name: Least Privilege
models.first_principle: Every component should have access only to the resources it needs to perform its function, and no more. Excess privilege is an attack surface.
models.derived_from: Jerome Saltzer & Michael Schroeder, 1975; minimizing access scope minimizes blast radius when any component is compromised
models.when_applies: IAM roles, agent tool permissions, API scopes, database access, service accounts, multi-tenant data access
models.when_breaks_down: Administrative tasks that genuinely require broad access; emergency break-glass scenarios; bootstrapping a system before least-privilege boundaries are established
models.ai_application: An AI agent with access to every tool in your catalog can cause catastrophic harm when it hallucinates a tool call. Give each agent a minimal, task-specific toolset. A document summariser should not have write access to production databases.

models.name: Zero Trust
models.first_principle: Never trust, always verify. No component should be trusted by virtue of its network location or identity alone — every request must be authenticated and authorized.
models.derived_from: John Kindervag, 2010; a response to the failure of perimeter-based security models where internal traffic was implicitly trusted
models.when_applies: Multi-tenant systems, microservice authentication, agent-to-agent calls, API gateway design, any system where lateral movement is a threat
models.when_breaks_down: Fully isolated single-tenant systems with no external attack surface; when the cost of verification exceeds the risk of implicit trust
models.ai_application: In a multi-agent system, an agent receiving a tool call result should not assume it came from a trusted orchestrator — prompt injection can hijack the orchestrator. Verify caller identity and validate inputs at every boundary.

models.name: Blast Radius
models.first_principle: When something fails, limit how much of the system it can damage. Design explicit containment boundaries so a failure in one area cannot cascade to everything.
models.derived_from: Military engineering; the zone of destruction from an explosion is bounded by distance and barriers — applied to systems design as fault isolation
models.when_applies: Service isolation, cell-based architecture, multi-tenant isolation, agent sandboxing, database per service, canary deployments
models.when_breaks_down: Systems so tightly coupled that isolation is architecturally impossible; when the cost of isolation exceeds the risk of cascade
models.ai_application: A shared Bedrock Knowledge Base across all tenants means one tenant's bad document poisons everyone's retrieval. Per-tenant isolation (separate indices or strict metadata filtering) contains the blast radius.

models.name: Cell-Based Architecture
models.first_principle: Partition a system into independent, identically structured cells. Each cell serves a subset of traffic. A failure in one cell cannot affect others.
models.derived_from: Amazon/Netflix engineering; sharding by natural unit (tenant, region, user cohort) creates blast radius boundaries at infrastructure level
models.when_applies: Multi-tenant SaaS, regional deployments, high-availability architectures, systems where one tenant's load must not affect another's
models.when_breaks_down: Small-scale systems where cell overhead is unjustified; workloads with no natural sharding key
models.ai_application: A per-tenant cell in a multi-tenant RAG system gives each tenant isolated S3 buckets, Knowledge Base indices, and inference quotas. One tenant's indexing storm cannot degrade another's query latency.

models.name: Caching Hierarchy
models.first_principle: Every cache trades consistency for speed. The closer the cache is to the consumer, the faster it is and the more stale it can become. You must explicitly decide how much staleness you can tolerate.
models.derived_from: CPU cache hierarchy (L1/L2/L3/RAM/disk); the memory hierarchy is the canonical example of trading distance for freshness
models.when_applies: API response caching, embedding caches, KV store design, CDN strategy, database query caches, prompt/response caching
models.when_breaks_down: Data that must always be fresh (financial balances, inventory); caches that become so large they are effectively the source of truth
models.ai_application: Caching embeddings for frequently queried documents dramatically reduces Bedrock Titan API costs. But if a document is updated and the cache isn't invalidated, your RAG system returns answers grounded in stale content — silently.

models.name: Schema-on-Read vs. Write
models.first_principle: You can enforce structure when data is written (schema-on-write) or when it is read (schema-on-read). Write enforcement provides consistency; read enforcement provides flexibility.
models.derived_from: Data engineering / Hadoop era; data lakes vs. data warehouses; the tension between ingestion speed and query reliability
models.when_applies: Data pipeline design, document store choice, event schema evolution, AI knowledge base ingestion, multi-source data integration
models.when_breaks_down: Neither extreme is always right; most production systems are hybrid, enforcing a minimal write schema while tolerating optional fields at read time
models.ai_application: Ingesting heterogeneous documents into a RAG knowledge base is a schema-on-read problem — you don't know what structure the documents have at ingest time. But your output schema (what the agent returns to callers) should be strictly schema-on-write.

models.name: Law of Demeter
models.first_principle: A component should only talk to its immediate collaborators, not to collaborators of collaborators. Don't reach through objects to get to what you need.
models.derived_from: Ian Holland, 1987; 'only talk to your friends' — transitive dependencies create invisible coupling and propagate changes across unrelated modules
models.when_applies: API design, service mesh call patterns, agent orchestration, data model design, tool interface design
models.when_breaks_down: Facade patterns that deliberately aggregate deep access; data transfer objects explicitly designed to carry data across layers
models.ai_application: An orchestrator agent that directly manipulates a sub-agent's internal memory store is violating Demeter. If the memory store changes, the orchestrator breaks. Route all access through the sub-agent's declared interface.

models.name: Cynefin Framework
models.first_principle: Problems exist in four domains: Simple (apply best practice), Complicated (expert analysis), Complex (cause/effect only visible in retrospect — probe then act), Chaotic (act first, stabilize). Applying the wrong response to the domain causes harm.
models.derived_from: Dave Snowden, IBM; a sensemaking framework for deciding how to respond based on the nature of a problem's causality
models.when_applies: Architecture decision-making, incident response, deciding when to run experiments vs. apply known patterns, evaluating AI system behavior
models.when_breaks_down: When the domain is misidentified — the most common failure; in fast-moving situations where there's no time for domain assessment
models.ai_application: LLM behavior in production is Complex, not Complicated. You cannot analyze your way to the right system prompt — you must probe (run evals), observe the response, then adapt. Applying 'best practice' prompting to a novel domain without probing first is a category error.

models.name: Two-Way Door Decisions
models.first_principle: Distinguish reversible decisions (two-way doors — decide fast, course-correct later) from irreversible ones (one-way doors — decide slowly, with full information). Treating all decisions as one-way doors is the biggest cause of organisational slowness.
models.derived_from: Jeff Bezos, Amazon shareholder letters; the asymmetry of reversibility should determine how much process you apply to a decision
models.when_applies: Architecture decisions, technology choices, API contracts, data model design, team structure changes
models.when_breaks_down: When a decision that appears reversible creates hidden path dependencies that make reversal effectively impossible
models.ai_application: Choosing which embedding model to use for a Bedrock Knowledge Base is a one-way door — switching means re-embedding your entire corpus. Choosing a chunk size is a two-way door. Apply disproportionate rigor to the former.

models.name: Cognitive Load Theory
models.first_principle: Human working memory is limited to 4±1 chunks simultaneously. System complexity that exceeds this limit causes errors, not just discomfort. Complexity is a tax paid in mistakes.
models.derived_from: John Sweller, 1988; cognitive psychology applied to instructional design, and now widely applied to software design and API usability
models.when_applies: API design, system prompt design, onboarding documentation, code review, architecture diagram design, multi-step workflow design
models.when_breaks_down: Expert users with extensive domain models who have effectively cached complexity into long-term memory; automated systems with no human in the loop
models.ai_application: A system prompt with 20 numbered rules exceeds working memory for both the LLM (attention dilution) and the human maintaining it. Collapse rules into principles; use structured sections to chunk complexity. The model can't 'hold' every rule simultaneously any more than a human can.

models.name: Normalisation of Deviance
models.first_principle: When a system repeatedly violates safety norms without immediate failure, those violations become accepted as normal. The absence of disaster is mistaken for evidence of safety.
models.derived_from: Diane Vaughan's analysis of the Space Shuttle Challenger disaster; small acceptable deviations accumulate until catastrophic failure
models.when_applies: Production systems with accumulating tech debt, security exceptions that persist, AI systems with known but tolerated failure modes, manual override processes
models.when_breaks_down: Systems with rapid, clear feedback loops where every deviation is immediately visible; highly regulated environments with strict enforcement
models.ai_application: A RAG system that 'usually gets it right' creates normalised deviance around hallucinations. Teams stop filing bugs for 'minor' hallucinations. Over time, the acceptable error rate drifts upward and no one knows the true production failure rate.

models.name: Premature Optimisation
models.first_principle: Optimising before you know where the bottleneck is wastes effort and adds complexity. Make it correct first, then measure, then optimise the measured bottleneck.
models.derived_from: Donald Knuth: 'premature optimisation is the root of all evil'; optimisation without measurement targets the wrong thing 80% of the time
models.when_applies: Any engineering work — code, architecture, prompts, pipelines; especially early in a project before real usage patterns are known
models.when_breaks_down: Known, fixed bottlenecks that are predictable from first principles; systems where correctness and performance cannot be separated
models.ai_application: Spending two weeks optimising your embedding chunk size before you have 100 real user queries is premature optimisation. Instrument first, get real query patterns, then tune. Most RAG performance problems are in retrieval quality, not chunk size arithmetic.

models.name: Strangler Fig Pattern
models.first_principle: Incrementally replace a legacy system by routing new functionality through a new system alongside the old one, gradually shifting traffic until the old system can be retired.
models.derived_from: Martin Fowler; named after the strangler fig tree that grows around a host tree — a metaphor for incremental, safe migration that avoids a risky big-bang cutover
models.when_applies: Migrating monoliths to microservices, replacing legacy integrations, introducing AI into existing workflows, upgrading data pipelines without downtime
models.when_breaks_down: Systems so tightly coupled that a seam cannot be introduced; when the old and new systems cannot run in parallel without data consistency problems
models.ai_application: When introducing AI into an existing workflow (e.g., replacing a rules-based document classifier with an LLM), run both systems in parallel first. Shadow the AI output against the rules engine, measure divergence, build confidence, then cut over incrementally.

models.name: Context Window = Working Memory
models.first_principle: An LLM's context window is its only working memory. Everything the model knows about the current task must fit in that window. What isn't in the context doesn't exist to the model.
models.derived_from: Transformer architecture; attention is computed over the full context — the model has no persistent state between calls beyond what is explicitly provided
models.when_applies: Prompt engineering, multi-turn agent design, RAG architecture, conversation summarisation, long-document processing, agentic loop design
models.when_breaks_down: Models with explicit external memory tools; architectures where persistence is handled outside the model (e.g., Bedrock Agent Core Memory)
models.ai_application: An agentic loop that accumulates tool results in the context window will eventually overflow. Design explicit summarisation and eviction strategies — treat the context window like a finite buffer, not infinite scratch space.

models.name: Ground Truth Gap
models.first_principle: The quality of an AI system is bounded by the quality of its evaluation. Without reliable ground truth, you cannot distinguish a system that is improving from one that is getting better at fooling your metrics.
models.derived_from: Machine learning practice; the hardest part of building AI systems is not the model but the evaluation — labelled data is the scarcest resource
models.when_applies: Building evaluation harnesses for LLMs, designing RAG quality metrics, assessing agent correctness, fine-tuning data curation, red-teaming
models.when_breaks_down: Well-defined closed-world tasks where ground truth is unambiguous (e.g., code execution, mathematical proofs)
models.ai_application: Most RAG pipelines are evaluated by asking the LLM if its own answer is correct — an LLM grading its own work. This measures fluency, not factual correctness. Invest in human-labelled golden datasets for your domain before trusting automated metrics.

models.name: Temperature & Creativity
models.first_principle: Higher sampling temperature increases output diversity at the cost of reliability. Lower temperature increases consistency at the cost of creativity. There is no free lunch.
models.derived_from: Softmax temperature in language model sampling; scaling logits changes the probability distribution over tokens from peaked (deterministic) to flat (random)
models.when_applies: Choosing inference parameters, designing agent personas, building classification vs. generation systems, A/B testing prompt strategies
models.when_breaks_down: Tasks where determinism matters absolutely (use temperature=0); tasks where diversity is the sole goal and consistency is irrelevant
models.ai_application: A RAG system answering factual questions should use low temperature (0–0.3). An agent brainstorming architecture alternatives should use higher temperature (0.7–1.0). Using the same temperature for both is a common misconfiguration.

models.name: Toil vs. Investment
models.first_principle: Toil is manual, repetitive, automatable work that scales linearly with system size and produces no lasting value. Investment is work that reduces future toil. A system generating more toil than investment is compounding technical debt.
models.derived_from: Google SRE book; the 'toil budget' concept — SREs should spend no more than 50% of time on toil, or the system is unmanageable at scale
models.when_applies: Evaluating automation priorities, runbook design, deciding when to build tooling vs. do things manually, sizing on-call burden
models.when_breaks_down: One-off tasks that will never recur; situations where the cost of automation exceeds the lifetime toil it would eliminate
models.ai_application: Manually reviewing every AI-generated output before it reaches users is toil. It scales with request volume, not engineering headcount. Build automated evaluation pipelines early — they are investment that reduces toil as the system scales.

models.name: Reversibility & Entropy
models.first_principle: Systems naturally drift toward disorder over time. Without active investment in order, complexity accumulates, documentation goes stale, and technical debt compounds. Maintenance is not optional — it is the price of operation.
models.derived_from: Second law of thermodynamics applied to software; systems entropy was formalised by Manny Lehman's laws of software evolution
models.when_applies: Long-lived systems, legacy migration planning, documentation strategy, dependency management, prompt and knowledge base maintenance
models.when_breaks_down: Short-lived systems designed to be thrown away; systems with extremely stable domains that genuinely don't change
models.ai_application: A Bedrock Knowledge Base that isn't actively maintained drifts out of sync with reality. Documents become stale, embeddings represent old terminology, and the system confidently answers questions with outdated facts. Schedule entropy work — it's not optional.

models.name: Map-Territory Distinction
models.first_principle: The map is not the territory. Every model, diagram, schema, or abstraction is a simplified representation of reality, not reality itself. Acting as if the map is the territory causes systematic errors.
models.derived_from: Alfred Korzybski, 1931; the map analogy highlights that all mental models are lossy compressions of an underlying reality
models.when_applies: Architecture diagrams, data models, SLA definitions, benchmark results, AI system evaluations, capacity models
models.when_breaks_down: Formal systems (mathematics, logic) where the model IS the territory by definition
models.ai_application: An LLM's 'knowledge' is a map of the world at training time, not the world itself. When you ask Claude about a live system, it answers from its map, not from your reality. RAG is the mechanism for injecting current territory into the model's map.

models.name: Occam's Razor
models.first_principle: Among competing explanations or solutions of equal explanatory power, prefer the simplest. Complexity should not be added without a demonstrated need.
models.derived_from: William of Ockham, 14th century; the principle that entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity — now foundational to scientific inference and engineering design
models.when_applies: Architecture decisions, incident diagnosis, model selection, prompt design, explaining AI outputs, choosing between two solutions of similar efficacy
models.when_breaks_down: Problems with genuine irreducible complexity; when the simple explanation is demonstrably wrong and only a complex one fits the evidence
models.ai_application: When a RAG system returns a wrong answer, the simplest explanation (wrong chunk retrieved) is usually correct. Don't hypothesize model hallucination, embedding model failure, and chunking bugs simultaneously — test the simplest cause first.
